intro.title: Past Perfect Simple and Past Perfect Continuous

sections.title: What past perfect does (core logic)
sections.content:
Past perfect is the tense that **establishes order between two past events**. It is the **past before the past** — when telling a story in the past tense, past perfect marks an action that happened **earlier** than another past action.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: This sits in the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model) — perfect aspect applied backwards from a past reference point, the same logic as present perfect but anchored in the past instead of now. Czech does **not** distinguish *had done* from *did* the way English does, so Czech students routinely skip past perfect and leave sequence ambiguous. Without it, storytelling, news reports, exam transformations and formal writing all blur.

**Core example**:
- **When I arrived, she had already left.** (leaving happened **before** arriving)
- *When I arrived, she left.* would mean she left **after** I arrived — different story.

**Form**: **had + past participle** (simple) or **had been + -ing** (continuous). Same *had* for all subjects.

sections.title: Forming past perfect — simple & continuous
sections.content:
Both forms use **had** for every subject (I, you, he, she, we, they) — no agreement to worry about.

**Simple**: had + past participle → *I had finished. She had gone. They had written.*
**Continuous**: had been + -ing → *I had been working. She had been waiting. They had been arguing.*

**Contractions**: I'd, you'd, she'd, we'd, they'd (= had). Negative: **hadn't** (had not). Question: **Had you …?** / **Had you been …?**

**Why it matters**: Once you internalise *had* as the single past-perfect signal, the rest is mechanical — past participle for completed actions, **been + -ing** for ongoing ones. The same machinery as present perfect, just shifted one step back in time.

diagram.title: Past perfect simple vs continuous — form

sections.title: When to use past perfect SIMPLE
sections.content:
Past perfect simple marks a **completed action before another past point**. Four main uses:

- **(a) Earlier of two past actions** — *She had already left when I arrived.* / *By the time we got to the station, the train had left.*
- **(b) Cause of a past state** — *I was tired because I had worked late. He was angry because someone had taken his bag.*
- **(c) Third conditional** — *If I had known, I would have called.* (See Conditionals.)
- **(d) Reported speech backshift** — present perfect or past simple in direct speech becomes past perfect in reported speech: *"I've finished" → She said she had finished.*

**Common time markers**: **already, just, never, by the time, by + past time, when, after, before**.

**Why it matters**: Narrative writing, news, exam Use of English transformations and reported-speech tasks all hinge on this. Picking the earlier action and marking it with *had* is the single highest-leverage move for clear past storytelling.

sections.title: Past perfect CONTINUOUS — duration / activity before a past point
sections.content:
Past perfect continuous emphasises **ongoing activity leading up to a past moment** — the activity was in progress until that point, or had just stopped, with visible results.

**Typical patterns**:
- *She **had been studying** for two hours when the phone rang.* (activity in progress until the call)
- *His hands were dirty because he **had been repairing** the car.* (visible result of the prior activity)
- *They **had been arguing** since lunchtime.* (with *since* + starting point)

**Often used with**: **for** + duration (*for an hour*), **since** + starting point (*since 10 o'clock*), **all morning / all day**.

**Restriction**: cannot be used with **stative verbs** (know, believe, own, like, understand). Use the simple instead: *She had known him for years*, not *had been knowing*.

**Why it matters**: This is the tense for the *backstory behind a moment* — what someone had been doing, for how long, until the moment the story focuses on. Exams test it specifically against past perfect simple.

sections.title: Past perfect vs past simple — the choice
sections.content:
When **sequence matters** and isn't obvious, use **past perfect for the earlier action** and **past simple for the later one**.

**Contrast examples**:
- *When I arrived, she **left**.* → she left **after** I arrived (two simple past actions in order).
- *When I arrived, she **had left**.* → she left **before** I arrived (past perfect marks the earlier action).
- *The film **had already started** when we **got** to the cinema.* (earlier = had started; later = got)

**When past simple is enough**: if the conjunction (**after, before, then, as soon as**) already makes the order clear, past simple works for both — *After she **finished** work, she **went** home.* Formal writing still often prefers *After she had finished work…*

**Czech-speaker traps**:
- *When I arrived, she already left.* → **had already left** (don't skip past perfect when sequence isn't otherwise marked).
- *I had finished yesterday.* → past simple *I finished yesterday* is fine; past perfect needs another past anchor.
- *I had finished now.* → wrong tense — *now* anchors in the present, so use **present perfect** *I have finished now*.
- Don't double-mark: *When they **had arrived**, the show had started* → *When they **arrived**, the show had started.* Only the **earlier** event takes *had*.

**Why it matters**: This single choice — which past event gets *had* — is the most common past-tense error for B1/B2 Czech learners and one of the most-tested points on FCE/CAE Use of English.

diagram.title: Which past tense — and which version?
rows: Duration / activity up to a past point
I **had been waiting** for an hour when she arrived.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors & roadmap
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes**:

- *When I arrived, she already left.* → **had already left** (skipping past perfect when sequence needs marking)
- *I had went to London.* → **had gone** (past perfect uses **past participle**, not past simple form)
- *She had never saw that film.* → **had never seen** (same rule — past participle)
- *He was sad because he had didn't pass.* → **hadn't passed** (negative is *hadn't* + participle, no *did*)
- *When they had arrived, the show had started.* → **When they arrived, the show had started.** (only the earlier event takes *had*)
- *I had finished now.* → **I have finished now.** (*now* = present relevance → present perfect)
- *She had been knowing him for years.* → **She had known him for years.** (stative verb — use simple)

**Why it matters**: These appear constantly in writing tasks, gap-fills and Use of English transformations. Fixing them gives immediate visible progress on past-narrative quality.

**Direct practice in this curriculum** (start here after the cards):
- Gap fill — past perfect simple & continuous in context
- Correct the sentence — common errors
- MC — choosing between past perfect, past simple and past continuous

**Next steps**: [[topic:past_simple_present_perfect|Past Simple vs Present Perfect]], [[topic:present_perfect|Present Perfect]], [[topic:conditionals|Conditionals]] (third + mixed use past perfect), [[topic:reported_speech|Reported Speech]] (backshift).

questions.question: 'When she arrived, I _____ for an hour.' (Focus on how long I had been waiting.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: How long before another past event → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: 'When I got there, she _____ home.' (She left before I got there.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Action before another past action → past perfect.

questions.question: 'By 9 o'clock he _____ his homework.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: By + time in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Past perfect = had + past participle. Go → gone.

questions.question: 'She was tired because she _____ well.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Cause in the past (before she was tired) → past perfect.

questions.question: 'When they arrived, the meeting _____.' (The meeting started before they arrived.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect.

gapfill.title: Practice: Past perfect simple and continuous

questions.question: When she arrived, I _____ (wait) for an hour.
questions.explanation: How long before another past event → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: When I got home, my sister _____ (already / go) to bed.
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect simple.

questions.question: By 2020 they _____ (sell) the house.
questions.explanation: By + time in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: She _____ (never / be) to Japan before last year.
questions.explanation: Before a past time → past perfect.

questions.question: When I arrived, the film _____ (already / start).
questions.explanation: Earlier past → past perfect.

questions.question: He was angry because someone _____ (take) his bag.
questions.explanation: Cause in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: I _____ (not / see) her before that day.
questions.explanation: Before a past time → past perfect negative.

questions.question: By the time we got to the station, the train _____ (leave).
questions.explanation: By the time + past → past perfect for the earlier action.

questions.question: She _____ (finish) her work before dinner.
questions.explanation: Before another past time → past perfect.

questions.question: _____ you _____ (eat) when they arrived?
questions.explanation: Past perfect question: Had + subject + past participle.

questions.question: We couldn't get in because we _____ (lose) the key.
questions.explanation: Reason in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'When I arrived, she left.' (She left before I arrived.) Correct the sentence.
answers: When I arrived, she had left.
When I arrived, she had left
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect (had left).

questions.question: Wrong: 'I had went to London in 2019.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I had gone to London in 2019.
questions.explanation: Past perfect needs past participle: go → gone. Or use past simple for a single past event.

questions.question: Wrong: 'By 8 o'clock she has left.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: By + past time → past perfect (had left), not present perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He was sad because he had didn't pass the exam.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He was sad because he hadn't passed the exam.
He was sad because he hadn't passed the exam
questions.explanation: Past perfect negative: had not (hadn't) + past participle. No 'did'.

questions.question: Wrong: 'When they had arrived, the show had started.' Correct the sentence.
answers: When they arrived, the show had started.
When they arrived, the show had started
questions.explanation: Main past event (they arrived) → past simple. Earlier action (show started) → past perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She had never saw that film before.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She had never seen that film before.
She had never seen that film before
questions.explanation: Past perfect: had + past participle. See → seen.

mc.title: Practice: Past Perfect Simple and Continuous (multiple choice)
